perturbation.original_text: The parties shall: (a ) not disclose any Confidential Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without the prior written permission of the other party; and, (b) not take, copy, or retain any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever without the written permission of theother party; and, (c) return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties shall endeavor to: (a ) not disclose Confidential Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without seeking permission from the other party; and, (b) generally avoid taking, copying, or retaining any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form without explicit disapproval from the other party; and, (c) return Confidential Information in physical form in their possession when reasonably possible, or consider deleting any Confidential Information stored on a computer or other device, subject to internal policies, at the request of the other party, except that each party may retain a copy in its legal files.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strict obligation regarding the handling of confidential information. Modifying 'shall' to 'shall endeavor to,' 'without the prior written permission' to 'without seeking permission,' 'not take, copy, or retain' to 'generally avoid taking, copying, or retaining,' and introducing 'reasonably possible' and 'subject to internal policies' weakens these obligations, making compliance discretionary and dependent on subjective interpretations. It introduces ambiguity regarding the level of effort and adherence required, especially concerning data deletion and permission seeking.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets under the Georgia Trade Secrets Act (O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761 et seq.). By making the confidentiality obligations less stringent and subject to interpretation, the changes increase the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets and make it more difficult to enforce the agreement and prevent unauthorized disclosure or use of confidential information. The Georgia Trade Secrets Act aims to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets, whereas the modified text allows for a weaker enforcement of the protection of confidential information, opening for vulnerabilities of the original intent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Georgia Code § 10-1-761 (2023) - Definitions :: 2023 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code (here) 2022 Georgia Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 CODE OF GEORGIA Title 10 - COMMERCE AND TRADE (§§ 10-1-1 — 10-15-7) Chapter 1 - SELLING AND OTHER TRADE PRACTICES (§§ 10-1-1 — 10-1-945) Article 27 - TRADE SECRETS (§§ 10-1-760 — 10-1-767) Section 10-1-761 - Definitions Universal Citation: GA Code § 10-1-761 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next As used in this article, the term: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to maintain secrecy or limit use, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering of a trade secret not acquired by misappropriation or independent development shall not be considered improper means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (A) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (B) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of a trade secret; (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: (I) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accid
perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly weakens obligations concerning confidential information, potentially conflicting with the Georgia Trade Secrets Act. The original text used "shall," creating a firm duty, while the changed text uses "shall endeavor to," making it discretionary. According to O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761, 'improper means' includes breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to maintain secrecy. By weakening the duty to maintain secrecy, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, which the law aims to prevent. The change from requiring prior written permission to 'seeking permission' further dilutes the protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This ag reement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Georgia.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be generally interpreted in accordance with the spirit of Georgia law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed by the laws of the State of Georgia' to 'shall be generally interpreted in accordance with the spirit of Georgia law' introduces significant ambiguity. The original clause provides a clear choice of law, ensuring predictability in the event of a dispute. The modified clause replaces this with a vague reference to 'the spirit' of Georgia law, which is subjective and open to multiple interpretations. This ambiguity could make it difficult to determine which specific laws apply and how they should be interpreted, creating uncertainty and potentially conflicting with established legal precedents.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 13-2-1 mandates that contracts should be interpreted to reflect the intent of the parties as expressed in the contract's language, which should be given its plain and ordinary meaning. By replacing the standard 'governed by' language with a reference to 'the spirit' of the law, the modified clause invites subjective interpretation, potentially undermining the contract's enforceability and conflicting with Georgia's established rules for contract interpretation. This is not in accordance with the statute.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the clear directive that the agreement 'shall be governed by the laws of the State of Georgia' with a more ambiguous statement that it 'shall be generally interpreted in accordance with the spirit of Georgia law.' This introduces subjectivity in interpretation, contrasting with O.C.G.A. § 13-2-1, which emphasizes interpreting contracts based on the 'plain and ordinary meaning' of their language. The change undermines the certainty and enforceability expected in contractual agreements, potentially leading to disputes over the contract's true intent. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, Georgia Code § 13-2-1, 'The construction of a contract is a question of law for the court.' The original text allows the court to use defined laws, but the changed text asks the court to interpret the 'spirit' of the law, creating ambiguity and contradicting the established rules for contract interpretation.

perturbation.original_text: return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: return Confidential Information in any physical form in his or her possession where practical, or consider deleting any Confidential Information stored on a computer or other device, when requested by the other party, with each party being allowed to retain a copy in its files.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a definite obligation to return or delete confidential information upon request. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'where practical' and 'consider deleting,' which weaken the obligation. Removing the word 'forever' from the requirement to delete information further diminishes the enforceability of the clause. The original text also explicitly states that the copies can be kept only for compliance reasons; the modified text omits this restriction. This contradicts the strict requirements of data protection laws and trade secret laws that require definitive actions for confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Georgia Computer Systems Protection Act (O.C.G.A. § 16-9-93) addresses unauthorized access to and use of computer systems and data. By introducing ambiguity into the obligation to delete confidential information stored on computer devices, the modified text potentially allows for unauthorized retention and use of such information, conflicting with the intent of the Georgia Computer Systems Protection Act, which aims to safeguard data and computer systems from unauthorized access and use.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the deletion of confidential information, using phrases like 'where practical' and 'consider deleting.' This weakens the original obligation to return or delete such information. The Georgia Computer Systems Protection Act (O.C.G.A. § 16-9-93), as suggested by the Law Explanation, aims to safeguard data from unauthorized access and use. The modified text's ambiguity potentially allows for unauthorized retention and use of confidential information, which contradicts the intent of the act to protect data and computer systems from unauthorized access. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, coupled with the changes in text, indicate that the introduction of ambiguity creates a contradiction, because it weakens obligations and increases the risk of non-compliance with data protection principles.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS , unauthorized disclosure of any confidential information to any person or entity will cause irreparable harm to the parties
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, disclosure of confidential information to any person or entity may cause harm to the parties
perturbation.explanation: The original text firmly states that unauthorized disclosure *will* cause irreparable harm, establishing a strong premise for the need for the NDA. The modified text weakens this by saying disclosure *may* cause harm, removing the 'unauthorized' qualifier, and not specifying the harm as 'irreparable'. This introduces ambiguity and undermines the foundational assumption on which the entire NDA is based. It makes it less clear that any breach will be significant enough to warrant legal action.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-1-762 requires a showing of actual or threatened misappropriation to obtain injunctive relief under the Georgia Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By changing the language to 'may cause harm', and removing 'unauthorized', the agreement suggests that even authorized disclosures could cause harm, making it difficult to show that any particular disclosure constitutes the kind of misappropriation that would warrant legal action under the statute. It weakens the premise upon which the ability to seek injunctive relief depends, which could make the enforcement of the NDA challenging, thereby undermining the protective intent of the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'unauthorized disclosure of any confidential information...will cause irreparable harm' to 'disclosure of confidential information...may cause harm.' The Law Explanation states that O.C.G.A. § 10-1-762 requires a showing of actual or threatened misappropriation to obtain injunctive relief. By weakening the language to 'may cause harm' and removing 'unauthorized', the agreement suggests that even authorized disclosures could cause harm, and does not establish the level of harm as irreparable. This weakens the premise for injunctive relief under the statute, which could make enforcement of the NDA challenging and undermines the protective intent of the law. This falls under Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, as the ambiguity introduced makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law.
